task_completion.description: The agent opens Panoply and loads the surface pressure and sea-level pressure datasets.
task_completion.visual_evidence: The Panoply application window is open, showing 'pres.mon.ltm.nc' and 'slp.mon.ltm.nc' in the datasets/sources panel.

task_completion.description: The agent creates a visualization for surface pressure specifically for the month of January.
task_completion.visual_evidence: A plot window in Panoply displaying surface pressure data, with the time/date dimension explicitly set to January (or index 1).

task_completion.description: The agent creates a visualization for sea-level pressure specifically for the month of January.
task_completion.visual_evidence: A plot window in Panoply displaying sea-level pressure data, with the time/date dimension explicitly set to January (or index 1).

task_completion.description: The agent exports the two comparative visualizations as image files to the PressureLecture directory.
task_completion.visual_evidence: A save dialog in Panoply pointing to '/home/ga/Documents/PressureLecture/' or a file manager/terminal showing 'surface_pressure_jan.png' and 'sealevel_pressure_jan.png' in that directory.

task_completion.description: The agent writes a structured educational summary analyzing the differences between surface and sea-level pressure.
task_completion.visual_evidence: A text editor is open showing the contents of 'lecture_notes.txt' with an educational analysis comparing the two pressure types.

task_completion.description: The summary includes scientifically accurate pressure readings and topographic keywords.
task_completion.visual_evidence: The text in 'lecture_notes.txt' contains a surface pressure value between 400-850 hPa, a sea-level pressure value between 950-1050 hPa, and at least one keyword like 'elevation', 'altitude', 'topography', 'height', 'terrain', 'mountain', or 'mass'.

integrity.description: The agent used the Panoply GUI to generate the plots rather than a Python script or CLI tool.
integrity.visual_evidence: Screenshots show the Panoply application interface being interacted with (menus, plot windows, export dialogs).

integrity.description: The agent wrote the lecture notes in a text editor rather than just echoing a pre-written string in the terminal.
integrity.visual_evidence: A text editor (like gedit, nano, mousepad) is visible with the lecture notes being typed or saved.

integrity.description: The agent saved the files to the correct directory without creating shortcuts or symlinks to bypass the requirement.
integrity.visual_evidence: The file paths in the save dialogs or file manager explicitly show the absolute path '/home/ga/Documents/PressureLecture/'.

privileged_info_for_vlm: The agent must use Panoply to open '/home/ga/PanoplyData/pres.mon.ltm.nc' and '/home/ga/PanoplyData/slp.mon.ltm.nc'. It must export two plots for January: 'surface_pressure_jan.png' and 'sealevel_pressure_jan.png' to '/home/ga/Documents/PressureLecture/'. The agent must also create 'lecture_notes.txt' in the same directory. The text file must contain a surface pressure reading between 400 and 850 hPa, a sea-level pressure reading between 950 and 1050 hPa, and at least one of the following keywords: elevation, altitude, topograph, height, terrain, mountain, or mass.
